The combination of rising rents and dwindling room availability has significant implications for tenants. Those already in flatshares may face rent hikes when their leases come up for renewal, while new renters could struggle to find suitable accommodation within their budget. For many, this situation may necessitate careful financial planning and prioritisation of housing needs.

It could be worth exploring options such as negotiating rent terms with landlords, considering less central locations, or sharing with more housemates to reduce individual costs. Additionally, staying informed about local rental trends and acting quickly when suitable rooms become available might help mitigate some of the challenges in this competitive market.

As the flatshare rental landscape continues to evolve, renters will need to adapt to these changing conditions. Keeping a close eye on market developments and being proactive in their housing search could make navigating this challenging environment more manageable.

item.title: Do Pre-2026 Tenancy Agreements Still Allow One Month’s Notice?
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The Renters' Rights Act 2025 brought significant changes to tenancy rules in England, but some uncertainty remains about how these affect agreements signed before the reforms took effect on 1st May 2026. One particular grey area involves notice periods for tenants wishing to end their tenancy, with many landlords and renters questioning whether older agreements still permit one month's notice.

Notice Period Rules Before 2026

Prior to the Renters' Rights Act, most periodic tenancies operated on a monthly basis, with notice periods typically matching the rental period. This meant:

  • Monthly tenancies generally required one month's notice
  • Quarterly tenancies typically needed three months' notice
  • Weekly tenancies required four weeks' notice under the Protection from Eviction Act

Many tenancy agreements from this period explicitly stated these notice requirements, which were considered both legally sound and consumer-friendly at the time. The rules aimed to create fairness by ensuring notice periods aligned with how frequently rent was paid.

Changes Introduced by the Renters' Rights Act

The 2025 legislation introduced a standard two-month notice period for tenants wishing to end their tenancy, unless both parties agree otherwise in writing. This change applies automatically to all new tenancies created after 1st May 2026.

However, the situation becomes less clear for ongoing tenancies that began before this date. While the new rules don't explicitly invalidate existing agreements, some legal experts suggest the two-month requirement might supersede previous terms unless both landlord and tenant actively agree to maintain the original notice period.

What This Means for Existing Tenancies

For those with pre-2026 agreements, it could be worth reviewing the notice terms carefully. While some older contracts might still technically permit one month's notice, tenants and landlords might consider discussing potential adjustments to align with current legislation.

Those uncertain about their rights may wish to seek professional advice, as the interpretation could depend on specific contract wording and whether either party has already referenced the new legislation in correspondence. The government's guidance on the Renters' Rights Act may also provide useful clarification.

As these changes continue to bed in, both landlords and tenants would benefit from clear communication about notice expectations to avoid potential disputes when ending a tenancy.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The Renters' Rights Act 2025 brought significant changes to tenancy rules in England, but some uncertainty remains about how these affect agreements signed before the reforms took effect on 1st May 2026. One particular grey area involves notice periods for tenants wishing to end their tenancy, with many landlords and renters questioning whether older agreements still permit one month's notice.

Notice Period Rules Before 2026

Prior to the Renters' Rights Act, most periodic tenancies operated on a monthly basis, with notice periods typically matching the rental period. This meant:

  • Monthly tenancies generally required one month's notice
  • Quarterly tenancies typically needed three months' notice
  • Weekly tenancies required four weeks' notice under the Protection from Eviction Act

Many tenancy agreements from this period explicitly stated these notice requirements, which were considered both legally sound and consumer-friendly at the time. The rules aimed to create fairness by ensuring notice periods aligned with how frequently rent was paid.

Changes Introduced by the Renters' Rights Act

The 2025 legislation introduced a standard two-month notice period for tenants wishing to end their tenancy, unless both parties agree otherwise in writing. This change applies automatically to all new tenancies created after 1st May 2026.

However, the situation becomes less clear for ongoing tenancies that began before this date. While the new rules don't explicitly invalidate existing agreements, some legal experts suggest the two-month requirement might supersede previous terms unless both landlord and tenant actively agree to maintain the original notice period.

What This Means for Existing Tenancies

For those with pre-2026 agreements, it could be worth reviewing the notice terms carefully. While some older contracts might still technically permit one month's notice, tenants and landlords might consider discussing potential adjustments to align with current legislation.

Those uncertain about their rights may wish to seek professional advice, as the interpretation could depend on specific contract wording and whether either party has already referenced the new legislation in correspondence. The government's guidance on the Renters' Rights Act may also provide useful clarification.

As these changes continue to bed in, both landlords and tenants would benefit from clear communication about notice expectations to avoid potential disputes when ending a tenancy.

item.title: What Makes Log Houses in Ireland Attractive to Investors
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Log houses in Ireland are gaining attention from property investors seeking sustainable and cost-effective alternatives to traditional brick-and-mortar homes. With growing interest in eco-conscious living and efficient construction methods, these timber-built properties present several potential advantages worth exploring.

Why Investors Are Considering Log Houses

Several factors are driving increased interest in log house investments across Ireland. Energy efficiency stands out as a key benefit, with timber's natural insulation properties helping to reduce heating costs—a significant advantage given rising energy prices. The construction process itself also tends to be quicker than conventional builds, potentially allowing investors to generate rental income sooner.

Market trends suggest growing consumer demand for sustainable housing options. Younger buyers and tenants in particular often favour properties with lower environmental impact. Log houses, when built using responsibly sourced timber, can appeal to this demographic while meeting stricter environmental regulations coming into force across the property sector.

Financial and Practical Considerations

From an investment perspective, log houses may offer certain financial benefits. Their typically lower construction costs compared to traditional homes could improve rental yields. Some investors also find these properties command premium rental values due to their unique aesthetic and eco-friendly credentials.

However, prospective buyers should carefully research:

  • Mortgage availability—some lenders may have specific requirements for non-standard construction
  • Insurance considerations—premiums can vary for timber-built properties
  • Long-term maintenance needs—while modern treatments improve durability, wood requires different upkeep to brick

For those considering this investment route, consulting both a mortgage broker experienced with alternative constructions and a surveyor specialising in timber properties could prove valuable. The Irish property market's continued evolution suggests log houses may become an increasingly mainstream option in coming years.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Log houses in Ireland are gaining attention from property investors seeking sustainable and cost-effective alternatives to traditional brick-and-mortar homes. With growing interest in eco-conscious living and efficient construction methods, these timber-built properties present several potential advantages worth exploring.

Why Investors Are Considering Log Houses

Several factors are driving increased interest in log house investments across Ireland. Energy efficiency stands out as a key benefit, with timber's natural insulation properties helping to reduce heating costs—a significant advantage given rising energy prices. The construction process itself also tends to be quicker than conventional builds, potentially allowing investors to generate rental income sooner.

Market trends suggest growing consumer demand for sustainable housing options. Younger buyers and tenants in particular often favour properties with lower environmental impact. Log houses, when built using responsibly sourced timber, can appeal to this demographic while meeting stricter environmental regulations coming into force across the property sector.

Financial and Practical Considerations

From an investment perspective, log houses may offer certain financial benefits. Their typically lower construction costs compared to traditional homes could improve rental yields. Some investors also find these properties command premium rental values due to their unique aesthetic and eco-friendly credentials.

However, prospective buyers should carefully research:

  • Mortgage availability—some lenders may have specific requirements for non-standard construction
  • Insurance considerations—premiums can vary for timber-built properties
  • Long-term maintenance needs—while modern treatments improve durability, wood requires different upkeep to brick

For those considering this investment route, consulting both a mortgage broker experienced with alternative constructions and a surveyor specialising in timber properties could prove valuable. The Irish property market's continued evolution suggests log houses may become an increasingly mainstream option in coming years.

item.title: Rental affordability improves in London as salary required falls
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

London Rental Affordability Shows Signs of Improvement

The financial burden of renting in London has eased slightly over the past year, with new data revealing a significant drop in the salary required to secure an average-priced home. According to Propertymark's Rental Price and Average Salary Tracker, the representative annual income needed to rent in the capital fell by 17% between June 2025 and June 2026 – from £86,250 to £71,550.

This positive trend extended beyond London, with the North West also recording a substantial 17.5% improvement in rental affordability. The required salary there dropped from £40,350 to £33,300 over the same period. Meanwhile, Scotland witnessed the most notable monthly rental price decline among UK regions, with average rents falling 5.7% from £1,257 to £1,186 between May and June.

Mixed Picture Across UK Rental Market

Regional variations continue to shape the UK rental landscape. London, the North West and South East all experienced monthly rent increases of 3.4%, 2.1% and 1.7% respectively. In contrast, Wales and the West Midlands saw modest declines of 0.4%, while Yorkshire and Humberside recorded a 1% drop.

Nationally, the average UK rent stood at £1,500 in June – a 2.3% annual increase. The typical salary required to rent an average-priced home across the country remained at £45,000. Kim Lidbury, ARLA Propertymark president, noted: "While rents continue to rise in some areas, other regions are experiencing more stable pricing or modest declines."

Lidbury added: "The supply of privately rented homes remains crucial to improving tenant choice and helping moderate rental costs in the long term." This observation comes as demand for quality rental properties continues to outstrip availability in many areas, maintaining upward pressure on prices despite recent affordability improvements.

For those considering their rental options, these regional variations highlight the importance of researching local market conditions. While some areas show improving affordability, the overall rental market remains competitive, particularly in high-demand regions.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

London Rental Affordability Shows Signs of Improvement

The financial burden of renting in London has eased slightly over the past year, with new data revealing a significant drop in the salary required to secure an average-priced home. According to Propertymark's Rental Price and Average Salary Tracker, the representative annual income needed to rent in the capital fell by 17% between June 2025 and June 2026 – from £86,250 to £71,550.

This positive trend extended beyond London, with the North West also recording a substantial 17.5% improvement in rental affordability. The required salary there dropped from £40,350 to £33,300 over the same period. Meanwhile, Scotland witnessed the most notable monthly rental price decline among UK regions, with average rents falling 5.7% from £1,257 to £1,186 between May and June.

Mixed Picture Across UK Rental Market

Regional variations continue to shape the UK rental landscape. London, the North West and South East all experienced monthly rent increases of 3.4%, 2.1% and 1.7% respectively. In contrast, Wales and the West Midlands saw modest declines of 0.4%, while Yorkshire and Humberside recorded a 1% drop.

Nationally, the average UK rent stood at £1,500 in June – a 2.3% annual increase. The typical salary required to rent an average-priced home across the country remained at £45,000. Kim Lidbury, ARLA Propertymark president, noted: "While rents continue to rise in some areas, other regions are experiencing more stable pricing or modest declines."

Lidbury added: "The supply of privately rented homes remains crucial to improving tenant choice and helping moderate rental costs in the long term." This observation comes as demand for quality rental properties continues to outstrip availability in many areas, maintaining upward pressure on prices despite recent affordability improvements.

For those considering their rental options, these regional variations highlight the importance of researching local market conditions. While some areas show improving affordability, the overall rental market remains competitive, particularly in high-demand regions.

item.title: Scottish Government failing to tackle housing emergency, says property firm
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Scotland’s housing crisis shows little sign of improvement, with new figures revealing housebuilding has dropped to its lowest level in over a decade. Recent statistics indicate a 4.4% decline in new build starts during 2025-26, raising concerns that the housing emergency declared in 2023 remains unresolved. Property experts warn the shortfall in new homes is exacerbating affordability pressures across the country.

Housebuilding slump continues across Scotland

The latest Scottish Government data shows just 14,955 new homes were started in 2025-26 – the lowest annual total since 2012-13. The private sector accounted for most of the decline, with starts falling by nearly 12% to 11,018 properties. While social housing saw a 25% increase, this still represents the third lowest output in 11 years.

Regional disparities remain stark, with Edinburgh accounting for over 12% of all new builds (1,850 homes) compared to just 831 in Glasgow. Industry analysts suggest this concentration of development could deepen existing inequalities in housing access between different parts of the country.

Calls for urgent government action

Property experts have criticised the lack of progress since Scotland’s housing emergency was officially declared. David Alexander of DJ Alexander Scotland commented: "Two years on, we’re seeing further reductions in construction rather than the necessary expansion. The private sector slowdown is particularly worrying."

The continuing shortfall in new homes is expected to have several knock-on effects:

  • Upward pressure on house prices as demand outstrips supply
  • Increased strain on the private rental sector
  • Growing waiting lists for social housing

With the current rate of housebuilding failing to meet Scotland’s needs, industry leaders are calling for immediate intervention to boost construction across all housing sectors. The coming years may prove critical in determining whether the country can reverse this downward trend and begin addressing its chronic housing shortage.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Scotland’s housing crisis shows little sign of improvement, with new figures revealing housebuilding has dropped to its lowest level in over a decade. Recent statistics indicate a 4.4% decline in new build starts during 2025-26, raising concerns that the housing emergency declared in 2023 remains unresolved. Property experts warn the shortfall in new homes is exacerbating affordability pressures across the country.

Housebuilding slump continues across Scotland

The latest Scottish Government data shows just 14,955 new homes were started in 2025-26 – the lowest annual total since 2012-13. The private sector accounted for most of the decline, with starts falling by nearly 12% to 11,018 properties. While social housing saw a 25% increase, this still represents the third lowest output in 11 years.

Regional disparities remain stark, with Edinburgh accounting for over 12% of all new builds (1,850 homes) compared to just 831 in Glasgow. Industry analysts suggest this concentration of development could deepen existing inequalities in housing access between different parts of the country.

Calls for urgent government action

Property experts have criticised the lack of progress since Scotland’s housing emergency was officially declared. David Alexander of DJ Alexander Scotland commented: "Two years on, we’re seeing further reductions in construction rather than the necessary expansion. The private sector slowdown is particularly worrying."

The continuing shortfall in new homes is expected to have several knock-on effects:

  • Upward pressure on house prices as demand outstrips supply
  • Increased strain on the private rental sector
  • Growing waiting lists for social housing

With the current rate of housebuilding failing to meet Scotland’s needs, industry leaders are calling for immediate intervention to boost construction across all housing sectors. The coming years may prove critical in determining whether the country can reverse this downward trend and begin addressing its chronic housing shortage.

item.title: Landlord exodus slows as market adjusts to Renters’ Rights Act
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The pace of landlords exiting the UK property market has slowed, according to recent data, as the sector begins to adapt to the Renters’ Rights Act introduced earlier this year. Analysis by estate agency Hamptons reveals that the proportion of homes listed for sale by landlords dropped to 9.2% in June, down from 11.3% in the same month last year. This suggests that the initial surge in landlord sales ahead of the legislation has eased, with earlier tax changes and rising mortgage costs being the primary drivers of previous exits.

Landlords Shift Focus to Purchases

June marked a significant shift in landlord activity, with purchases outpacing sales for the first time since 2019. Landlords accounted for 10.2% of property purchases during the month, compared to 9.2% of homes listed for sale that had previously been rented. This change highlights a growing trend of landlords reassessing their strategies in light of new regulations and market conditions. The Renters’ Rights Act, which came into force on 1st May, has introduced stricter rules for landlords considering a sale. For example, those using a Ground 1A notice to regain possession in order to sell now face a mandatory 12-month ban on re-letting the property, even if they fail to find a buyer.

Increased Risks for Landlords

The new legislation has altered the risk landscape for landlords, making selling a more complex proposition. Hamptons estimates that between 80,000 and 100,000 unsold rental homes would have been prevented from returning to the rental market for 12 months had the rules been in place last year. Aneisha Beveridge, head of research at Hamptons, noted that the balance of risk has shifted, with the prospect of holding onto an investment becoming more appealing for many landlords. “A tougher sales market and the introduction of a 12-month re-letting ban mean selling has become a more complicated proposition,” she said.

Regional Trends and Rental Growth

Landlord exits remain concentrated in London and the South of England, where higher property prices, lower yields, and elevated mortgage costs continue to impact investor returns. In London, 20.3% of homes listed for sale in June had previously been rented within the last five years, compared to 9.5% in the South East. Meanwhile, rental prices across Great Britain saw a notable increase in June, with newly let homes rising by 1.6% year-on-year to an average of £1,392 per month. Existing tenants experienced an average rent increase of 5.4%, with the North East recording the strongest growth at 4.3% year-on-year.

As the market adjusts to the Renters’ Rights Act and broader economic factors, landlords may find themselves weighing their options more carefully. While some may choose to hold onto their investments, others could explore alternative strategies to navigate the evolving landscape.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The pace of landlords exiting the UK property market has slowed, according to recent data, as the sector begins to adapt to the Renters’ Rights Act introduced earlier this year. Analysis by estate agency Hamptons reveals that the proportion of homes listed for sale by landlords dropped to 9.2% in June, down from 11.3% in the same month last year. This suggests that the initial surge in landlord sales ahead of the legislation has eased, with earlier tax changes and rising mortgage costs being the primary drivers of previous exits.

Landlords Shift Focus to Purchases

June marked a significant shift in landlord activity, with purchases outpacing sales for the first time since 2019. Landlords accounted for 10.2% of property purchases during the month, compared to 9.2% of homes listed for sale that had previously been rented. This change highlights a growing trend of landlords reassessing their strategies in light of new regulations and market conditions. The Renters’ Rights Act, which came into force on 1st May, has introduced stricter rules for landlords considering a sale. For example, those using a Ground 1A notice to regain possession in order to sell now face a mandatory 12-month ban on re-letting the property, even if they fail to find a buyer.

Increased Risks for Landlords

The new legislation has altered the risk landscape for landlords, making selling a more complex proposition. Hamptons estimates that between 80,000 and 100,000 unsold rental homes would have been prevented from returning to the rental market for 12 months had the rules been in place last year. Aneisha Beveridge, head of research at Hamptons, noted that the balance of risk has shifted, with the prospect of holding onto an investment becoming more appealing for many landlords. “A tougher sales market and the introduction of a 12-month re-letting ban mean selling has become a more complicated proposition,” she said.

Regional Trends and Rental Growth

Landlord exits remain concentrated in London and the South of England, where higher property prices, lower yields, and elevated mortgage costs continue to impact investor returns. In London, 20.3% of homes listed for sale in June had previously been rented within the last five years, compared to 9.5% in the South East. Meanwhile, rental prices across Great Britain saw a notable increase in June, with newly let homes rising by 1.6% year-on-year to an average of £1,392 per month. Existing tenants experienced an average rent increase of 5.4%, with the North East recording the strongest growth at 4.3% year-on-year.

As the market adjusts to the Renters’ Rights Act and broader economic factors, landlords may find themselves weighing their options more carefully. While some may choose to hold onto their investments, others could explore alternative strategies to navigate the evolving landscape.

item.title: Property videos deliver six times more impressions, marketer claims
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Professionally produced property videos could be a game-changer for estate agents looking to boost their online presence, according to recent data. A report by PropertyBox, a property marketing platform, reveals that agents using high-quality video content on social media achieve six times more impressions compared to those relying solely on static images. This trend highlights the growing importance of visual storytelling in the property market.

The Power of Video Marketing

PropertyBox’s analysis shows that agents utilising its platform generated over 8.1 million impressions and 212,000 website clicks in June 2026 alone. On average, each video post attracted 1,851 impressions, 701 views, and 48 clicks back to the agent’s website. These figures suggest that video content not only increases visibility but also drives meaningful engagement, helping agents build a dedicated audience.

The platform combines professional photography with artificial intelligence to create versatile social media content from a single property shoot. This approach allows agents to showcase listings across multiple platforms efficiently. By leveraging video, agents can capture attention in a crowded digital space, potentially increasing the chances of attracting serious buyers.

Boosting Sales and Sale Prices

Independent research by TwentyCi, cited by PropertyBox, supports the effectiveness of professional marketing. Properties marketed using high-quality content were found to be 28.3% more likely to complete a sale. Additionally, these properties achieved sale prices averaging 1.9% higher than comparable listings, translating to an extra £7,356 for sellers. This underscores the value of investing in professional marketing to maximise returns.

Lee Wainwright, Chief Executive of PropertyBox, emphasised the impact of video content, stating, “The agents everyone’s watching are the ones stopping the scroll and commanding the feeds with professionally shot video, not static posts.” He added that the platform’s videos drove over 212,000 clicks directly to agents’ websites in June, enabling them to grow their audience organically.

For homeowners considering selling their property, it could be worth exploring agents who prioritise professional marketing strategies. Similarly, estate agents might consider incorporating video content into their campaigns to enhance engagement and drive results.

As the property market becomes increasingly competitive, embracing innovative marketing techniques could prove essential for standing out. Video content, with its ability to captivate audiences and drive traffic, appears to be a powerful tool in achieving this goal.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Professionally produced property videos could be a game-changer for estate agents looking to boost their online presence, according to recent data. A report by PropertyBox, a property marketing platform, reveals that agents using high-quality video content on social media achieve six times more impressions compared to those relying solely on static images. This trend highlights the growing importance of visual storytelling in the property market.

The Power of Video Marketing

PropertyBox’s analysis shows that agents utilising its platform generated over 8.1 million impressions and 212,000 website clicks in June 2026 alone. On average, each video post attracted 1,851 impressions, 701 views, and 48 clicks back to the agent’s website. These figures suggest that video content not only increases visibility but also drives meaningful engagement, helping agents build a dedicated audience.

The platform combines professional photography with artificial intelligence to create versatile social media content from a single property shoot. This approach allows agents to showcase listings across multiple platforms efficiently. By leveraging video, agents can capture attention in a crowded digital space, potentially increasing the chances of attracting serious buyers.

Boosting Sales and Sale Prices

Independent research by TwentyCi, cited by PropertyBox, supports the effectiveness of professional marketing. Properties marketed using high-quality content were found to be 28.3% more likely to complete a sale. Additionally, these properties achieved sale prices averaging 1.9% higher than comparable listings, translating to an extra £7,356 for sellers. This underscores the value of investing in professional marketing to maximise returns.

Lee Wainwright, Chief Executive of PropertyBox, emphasised the impact of video content, stating, “The agents everyone’s watching are the ones stopping the scroll and commanding the feeds with professionally shot video, not static posts.” He added that the platform’s videos drove over 212,000 clicks directly to agents’ websites in June, enabling them to grow their audience organically.

For homeowners considering selling their property, it could be worth exploring agents who prioritise professional marketing strategies. Similarly, estate agents might consider incorporating video content into their campaigns to enhance engagement and drive results.

As the property market becomes increasingly competitive, embracing innovative marketing techniques could prove essential for standing out. Video content, with its ability to captivate audiences and drive traffic, appears to be a powerful tool in achieving this goal.

item.title: Next prime minister urged to prioritise leasehold reform amid slow sales
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Leasehold reform has been highlighted as a pressing issue for the next government, with property management firms urging action to address delays and difficulties in selling leasehold properties. According to experts, the current system is causing significant challenges for homeowners, lenders, and buyers, particularly in regions like the North of England.

The Challenges of Leasehold Properties

Leasehold properties, which are predominantly flats, now make up over half of the stock managed by some property firms. Issues such as shorter leases, escalating costs for lease extensions, rising ground rents, and increasing service charges are deterring potential buyers and mortgage lenders. Additionally, delays in obtaining essential management information from freeholders or management companies are further complicating transactions.

As leases shorten, properties become less appealing, and the cost of extending a lease can rise significantly. This has led to leasehold homes remaining on the market for an average of over 250 days—more than 100 days longer than freehold properties. Once an offer is accepted, leasehold sales also take around eight weeks longer to complete compared to freehold transactions.

The Impact on Homeowners and the Market

The North of England has been identified as a region where these issues are particularly prevalent. The combination of delays and failed sales has resulted in more leasehold properties being sold at auction, often at lower prices. This trend not only affects homeowners but also creates challenges for lenders and the wider property market.

While recent legislative reforms have been a step in the right direction, experts argue that more comprehensive changes are needed to address longstanding issues. These reforms could help streamline the process of buying and selling leasehold properties, making them more attractive to buyers and lenders alike.

A Call for Government Action

Property management firms are calling on the next prime minister to prioritise leasehold reform, particularly in regions like the North of England. They emphasise the need for momentum to be maintained, despite the frequent changes in leadership at Number 10. Meaningful reform could benefit thousands of leaseholders across the country, improving the efficiency and fairness of the property market.

As the government considers its priorities, homeowners, buyers, and lenders may wish to stay informed about potential changes to leasehold legislation. Addressing these challenges could lead to a more transparent and accessible property market for all involved.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Leasehold reform has been highlighted as a pressing issue for the next government, with property management firms urging action to address delays and difficulties in selling leasehold properties. According to experts, the current system is causing significant challenges for homeowners, lenders, and buyers, particularly in regions like the North of England.

The Challenges of Leasehold Properties

Leasehold properties, which are predominantly flats, now make up over half of the stock managed by some property firms. Issues such as shorter leases, escalating costs for lease extensions, rising ground rents, and increasing service charges are deterring potential buyers and mortgage lenders. Additionally, delays in obtaining essential management information from freeholders or management companies are further complicating transactions.

As leases shorten, properties become less appealing, and the cost of extending a lease can rise significantly. This has led to leasehold homes remaining on the market for an average of over 250 days—more than 100 days longer than freehold properties. Once an offer is accepted, leasehold sales also take around eight weeks longer to complete compared to freehold transactions.

The Impact on Homeowners and the Market

The North of England has been identified as a region where these issues are particularly prevalent. The combination of delays and failed sales has resulted in more leasehold properties being sold at auction, often at lower prices. This trend not only affects homeowners but also creates challenges for lenders and the wider property market.

While recent legislative reforms have been a step in the right direction, experts argue that more comprehensive changes are needed to address longstanding issues. These reforms could help streamline the process of buying and selling leasehold properties, making them more attractive to buyers and lenders alike.

A Call for Government Action

Property management firms are calling on the next prime minister to prioritise leasehold reform, particularly in regions like the North of England. They emphasise the need for momentum to be maintained, despite the frequent changes in leadership at Number 10. Meaningful reform could benefit thousands of leaseholders across the country, improving the efficiency and fairness of the property market.

As the government considers its priorities, homeowners, buyers, and lenders may wish to stay informed about potential changes to leasehold legislation. Addressing these challenges could lead to a more transparent and accessible property market for all involved.

item.title: North-South rental yield divide narrows, says lender
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The traditional gap between rental yields in northern and southern England has shown signs of narrowing, according to the latest market analysis from Fleet Mortgages. Their Q2 2026 Rental Barometer reveals that while regional variations persist, the differential between higher-yielding northern regions and their southern counterparts has become less pronounced.

Regional yield trends

Average rental yields across England and Wales stood at 7.8% in the second quarter of 2026, representing a 0.3 percentage point annual increase but a slight quarterly decline from 8.1%. The North East maintained its position as the highest-yielding region at 9.2%, despite experiencing a 0.6 percentage point decrease compared to Q1. Other northern regions saw modest declines, with Yorkshire and Humberside falling 0.3 percentage points and the West Midlands dropping 0.6 percentage points.

Meanwhile, London's rental yields showed resilience, increasing from 6.1% to 6.3%, while the South East remained stable at 6.9%. This gradual convergence suggests that while northern regions continue to offer stronger yields, the gap with southern markets is becoming less stark than in previous years.

Buy-to-let market activity

The report also highlighted positive signs in the buy-to-let sector, with purchase applications rising from 33% to 36% of total mortgage business between quarters. Portfolio landlords demonstrated particular activity, accounting for over 62% of applications. Limited company borrowing dominated the market, representing 78% of all buy-to-let mortgage applications.

Market conditions experienced turbulence early in the quarter due to geopolitical tensions affecting swap rates, but stabilised later in the period. This allowed lenders to reduce mortgage rates and reintroduce products that had been temporarily withdrawn during the period of uncertainty.

Steve Cox, Fleet Mortgages' chief commercial officer, noted: "The narrowing yield gap suggests investment opportunities exist across a wider geographical spread than previously. Combined with improving mortgage market conditions and strong tenant demand, this creates a more positive environment for landlords."

For buy-to-let investors, these trends may indicate the value of reviewing portfolio strategies to account for changing regional dynamics and evolving mortgage market conditions.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The traditional gap between rental yields in northern and southern England has shown signs of narrowing, according to the latest market analysis from Fleet Mortgages. Their Q2 2026 Rental Barometer reveals that while regional variations persist, the differential between higher-yielding northern regions and their southern counterparts has become less pronounced.

Regional yield trends

Average rental yields across England and Wales stood at 7.8% in the second quarter of 2026, representing a 0.3 percentage point annual increase but a slight quarterly decline from 8.1%. The North East maintained its position as the highest-yielding region at 9.2%, despite experiencing a 0.6 percentage point decrease compared to Q1. Other northern regions saw modest declines, with Yorkshire and Humberside falling 0.3 percentage points and the West Midlands dropping 0.6 percentage points.

Meanwhile, London's rental yields showed resilience, increasing from 6.1% to 6.3%, while the South East remained stable at 6.9%. This gradual convergence suggests that while northern regions continue to offer stronger yields, the gap with southern markets is becoming less stark than in previous years.

Buy-to-let market activity

The report also highlighted positive signs in the buy-to-let sector, with purchase applications rising from 33% to 36% of total mortgage business between quarters. Portfolio landlords demonstrated particular activity, accounting for over 62% of applications. Limited company borrowing dominated the market, representing 78% of all buy-to-let mortgage applications.

Market conditions experienced turbulence early in the quarter due to geopolitical tensions affecting swap rates, but stabilised later in the period. This allowed lenders to reduce mortgage rates and reintroduce products that had been temporarily withdrawn during the period of uncertainty.

Steve Cox, Fleet Mortgages' chief commercial officer, noted: "The narrowing yield gap suggests investment opportunities exist across a wider geographical spread than previously. Combined with improving mortgage market conditions and strong tenant demand, this creates a more positive environment for landlords."

For buy-to-let investors, these trends may indicate the value of reviewing portfolio strategies to account for changing regional dynamics and evolving mortgage market conditions.

item.title: Deposit replacement provider reports 58% rise in first-half sales
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Demand for deposit replacement services has surged in the first half of 2026, with one provider reporting a 58% increase in sales compared to the same period last year. The growth highlights shifting preferences in the rental market as tenants and landlords explore alternatives to traditional cash deposits.

Build-to-rent sector drives adoption

Reposit, a leading deposit replacement provider, saw particularly strong growth in the build-to-rent sector, where sales more than doubled year-on-year. The company attributes this trend to the sector's professionalisation and willingness to adopt innovative solutions. Alongside this expansion, Reposit added 195 new letting agent partners to its network, broadening its market reach.

The service works by charging tenants a non-refundable fee equal to one week's rent instead of requiring a traditional five-week deposit. Landlords receive equivalent financial protection, with tenants remaining responsible for any property damage at the end of the tenancy. According to company figures, this approach saved tenants over £17.2m in upfront moving costs during the period, averaging £1,088 per tenancy.

Industry partnerships expand

Several strategic partnerships have supported the company's growth, including becoming Goodlord's exclusive deposit replacement partner in May. Additional integrations with platforms like Let Alliance and HomeLet's Vision+ system have made the product more accessible to agents and landlords. These collaborations come as the rental market adapts to changes brought by the Renters' Rights Act.

Ben Grech, Reposit's chief executive, commented: "Our growth and these new partnerships show the industry is embracing different ways of working. While we're pleased with our first-half performance, we see even greater potential moving forward." The company provided £34m of landlord protection in six months, including £12.5m coverage beyond what traditional deposits would offer.

With deposit alternatives gaining traction, both renters and property professionals might find it valuable to compare traditional and modern approaches to tenancy deposits when arranging new lettings.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Demand for deposit replacement services has surged in the first half of 2026, with one provider reporting a 58% increase in sales compared to the same period last year. The growth highlights shifting preferences in the rental market as tenants and landlords explore alternatives to traditional cash deposits.

Build-to-rent sector drives adoption

Reposit, a leading deposit replacement provider, saw particularly strong growth in the build-to-rent sector, where sales more than doubled year-on-year. The company attributes this trend to the sector's professionalisation and willingness to adopt innovative solutions. Alongside this expansion, Reposit added 195 new letting agent partners to its network, broadening its market reach.

The service works by charging tenants a non-refundable fee equal to one week's rent instead of requiring a traditional five-week deposit. Landlords receive equivalent financial protection, with tenants remaining responsible for any property damage at the end of the tenancy. According to company figures, this approach saved tenants over £17.2m in upfront moving costs during the period, averaging £1,088 per tenancy.

Industry partnerships expand

Several strategic partnerships have supported the company's growth, including becoming Goodlord's exclusive deposit replacement partner in May. Additional integrations with platforms like Let Alliance and HomeLet's Vision+ system have made the product more accessible to agents and landlords. These collaborations come as the rental market adapts to changes brought by the Renters' Rights Act.

Ben Grech, Reposit's chief executive, commented: "Our growth and these new partnerships show the industry is embracing different ways of working. While we're pleased with our first-half performance, we see even greater potential moving forward." The company provided £34m of landlord protection in six months, including £12.5m coverage beyond what traditional deposits would offer.

With deposit alternatives gaining traction, both renters and property professionals might find it valuable to compare traditional and modern approaches to tenancy deposits when arranging new lettings.

item.title: What London’s 90-Night Rule Means for Serviced Flat Investors
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

For property investors focusing on serviced accommodation in London, understanding the 90-night rule is crucial. This regulation, which limits the number of nights a property can be let out for short-term stays, carries significant implications. Breaching it could lead to enforcement action, hefty fines, or even a planning injunction halting operations. Here’s a closer look at what the rule entails, who it applies to, and how investors can structure their portfolios to stay compliant.

What is the 90-Night Rule?

The 90-night rule restricts the use of residential properties in London for short-term lets to a maximum of 90 nights per calendar year. Introduced by the Greater London Authority, this regulation aims to balance the growing demand for short-term rentals with the need to preserve housing availability for long-term residents. Properties used for short-term lets beyond this limit must obtain planning permission for change of use to a “temporary sleeping accommodation” category.

This rule applies to platforms like Airbnb, Booking.com, and other short-term rental services. It’s particularly relevant for investors in serviced apartments, as non-compliance can result in severe consequences, including fines of up to £20,000 per offence.

Who is Exempt from the Rule?

Certain types of accommodation are exempt from the 90-night rule. These include hotels, hostels, and properties that already have planning permission for short-term lets. Additionally, landlords who rent out their primary residence for fewer than 90 nights a year are also unaffected. However, investors operating serviced flats as a business must adhere strictly to the regulation unless they secure the necessary permissions.

It’s worth noting that some boroughs may have additional restrictions or requirements, so investors should always check local planning policies before proceeding.

Structuring Investments for Compliance

For serviced accommodation investors, staying within the 90-night limit requires careful planning. One approach is to diversify portfolios by including properties outside London or focusing on long-term rentals. Alternatively, investors might consider applying for planning permission to legally exceed the limit, though this process can be time-consuming and costly.

Another strategy is to partner with professional property management companies that specialise in navigating these regulations. These firms can help ensure compliance while maximising rental yields. Additionally, using technology to track booking nights and manage occupancy rates can prevent accidental breaches of the rule.

Navigating London’s 90-night rule demands a proactive approach for serviced accommodation investors. By understanding the regulation, identifying exemptions, and structuring investments thoughtfully, landlords can avoid penalties and operate successfully within the legal framework. Always consulting with property experts or legal advisors is advisable to ensure full compliance and protect your investment.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

For property investors focusing on serviced accommodation in London, understanding the 90-night rule is crucial. This regulation, which limits the number of nights a property can be let out for short-term stays, carries significant implications. Breaching it could lead to enforcement action, hefty fines, or even a planning injunction halting operations. Here’s a closer look at what the rule entails, who it applies to, and how investors can structure their portfolios to stay compliant.

What is the 90-Night Rule?

The 90-night rule restricts the use of residential properties in London for short-term lets to a maximum of 90 nights per calendar year. Introduced by the Greater London Authority, this regulation aims to balance the growing demand for short-term rentals with the need to preserve housing availability for long-term residents. Properties used for short-term lets beyond this limit must obtain planning permission for change of use to a “temporary sleeping accommodation” category.

This rule applies to platforms like Airbnb, Booking.com, and other short-term rental services. It’s particularly relevant for investors in serviced apartments, as non-compliance can result in severe consequences, including fines of up to £20,000 per offence.

Who is Exempt from the Rule?

Certain types of accommodation are exempt from the 90-night rule. These include hotels, hostels, and properties that already have planning permission for short-term lets. Additionally, landlords who rent out their primary residence for fewer than 90 nights a year are also unaffected. However, investors operating serviced flats as a business must adhere strictly to the regulation unless they secure the necessary permissions.

It’s worth noting that some boroughs may have additional restrictions or requirements, so investors should always check local planning policies before proceeding.

Structuring Investments for Compliance

For serviced accommodation investors, staying within the 90-night limit requires careful planning. One approach is to diversify portfolios by including properties outside London or focusing on long-term rentals. Alternatively, investors might consider applying for planning permission to legally exceed the limit, though this process can be time-consuming and costly.

Another strategy is to partner with professional property management companies that specialise in navigating these regulations. These firms can help ensure compliance while maximising rental yields. Additionally, using technology to track booking nights and manage occupancy rates can prevent accidental breaches of the rule.

Navigating London’s 90-night rule demands a proactive approach for serviced accommodation investors. By understanding the regulation, identifying exemptions, and structuring investments thoughtfully, landlords can avoid penalties and operate successfully within the legal framework. Always consulting with property experts or legal advisors is advisable to ensure full compliance and protect your investment.

item.title: Landlord Law Newsround #444
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The government has unveiled further details about its upcoming landlord ombudsman scheme, which will extend the existing Housing Ombudsman Service currently serving social housing tenants. The new system aims to create consistency across both private and social rental sectors, with binding compensation awards of up to £25,000 available to tenants. While no exact start date has been confirmed, housing minister Baroness Taylor of Stevenage has promised landlords will receive adequate notice before mandatory participation begins.

Heatwave risks bring new responsibilities for landlords

Landlords could face fines of up to £7,000 if their rental properties become dangerously hot during heatwaves. Under the Housing Health and Safety Rating System (HHSRS), overheating that creates health risks may lead to penalties, particularly where ventilation systems are faulty. Whilst there's no legal maximum temperature requirement, broken extractor fans, jammed windows or other ventilation failures that compromise air flow could trigger enforcement action. Property experts emphasise that the key factor isn't a specific temperature threshold, but whether conditions become hazardous enough to affect tenants' wellbeing.

Disability discrimination considerations in eviction cases

With Section 21 'no fault' evictions being phased out, landlords relying on rent arrears or anti-social behaviour claims should be aware of potential disability discrimination counterclaims. Under the Equality Act 2010, tenants may argue that their disability contributed to any payment issues or behavioural problems. Courts must consider such arguments when assessing possession cases, meaning landlords need to carefully document all communications and demonstrate reasonable adjustments have been made where appropriate.

These developments highlight the evolving responsibilities facing landlords in England's private rental sector. Staying informed about regulatory changes and maintaining thorough property records could help landlords navigate these new requirements whilst protecting their interests.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The government has unveiled further details about its upcoming landlord ombudsman scheme, which will extend the existing Housing Ombudsman Service currently serving social housing tenants. The new system aims to create consistency across both private and social rental sectors, with binding compensation awards of up to £25,000 available to tenants. While no exact start date has been confirmed, housing minister Baroness Taylor of Stevenage has promised landlords will receive adequate notice before mandatory participation begins.

Heatwave risks bring new responsibilities for landlords

Landlords could face fines of up to £7,000 if their rental properties become dangerously hot during heatwaves. Under the Housing Health and Safety Rating System (HHSRS), overheating that creates health risks may lead to penalties, particularly where ventilation systems are faulty. Whilst there's no legal maximum temperature requirement, broken extractor fans, jammed windows or other ventilation failures that compromise air flow could trigger enforcement action. Property experts emphasise that the key factor isn't a specific temperature threshold, but whether conditions become hazardous enough to affect tenants' wellbeing.

Disability discrimination considerations in eviction cases

With Section 21 'no fault' evictions being phased out, landlords relying on rent arrears or anti-social behaviour claims should be aware of potential disability discrimination counterclaims. Under the Equality Act 2010, tenants may argue that their disability contributed to any payment issues or behavioural problems. Courts must consider such arguments when assessing possession cases, meaning landlords need to carefully document all communications and demonstrate reasonable adjustments have been made where appropriate.

These developments highlight the evolving responsibilities facing landlords in England's private rental sector. Staying informed about regulatory changes and maintaining thorough property records could help landlords navigate these new requirements whilst protecting their interests.

item.title: Property professionals offered free AML webinar
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Property professionals looking to stay up to date with evolving anti-money laundering (AML) requirements may benefit from a new free webinar. Hosted by FCS Compliance, the 30-minute session aims to clarify recent changes to Money Laundering Regulations and their practical impact on estate and letting agents.

Understanding the AML updates

The webinar, scheduled for Thursday 16 July at 1pm, comes shortly after HM Treasury announced amendments to the Money Laundering Regulations on 30 June. With further guidance expected soon, the session could help property businesses prepare for upcoming compliance obligations. Stephen Williamson, Lead AML Consultant at FCS Compliance, will guide attendees through the key changes and their implications.

Topics will include how the updated regulations affect estate agents' daily operations, along with anticipated HMRC expectations regarding risk assessments and customer due diligence. The session will particularly focus on practical adjustments that firms might need to make to their existing AML processes.

Key areas of focus

Property professionals attending the webinar can expect detailed coverage of several critical compliance areas:

  • The specific impact of regulation changes on estate and letting agents
  • How forthcoming HMRC guidance may influence AML compliance practices
  • Required updates to risk assessments, policies and procedures
  • When to review customer due diligence and enhanced due diligence measures
  • Identifying high-risk clients and transaction warning signs
  • Essential record-keeping, training and audit trail requirements

With HMRC increasing its focus on AML compliance in the property sector, the webinar may prove particularly valuable for businesses seeking to strengthen their controls. The session will offer actionable steps to help firms prepare for potential inspections whilst maintaining robust anti-money laundering processes.

Those interested in attending the free webinar can register through FCS Compliance's website. As regulatory requirements continue to evolve, staying informed about AML obligations remains crucial for all property professionals operating in the UK market.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Property professionals looking to stay up to date with evolving anti-money laundering (AML) requirements may benefit from a new free webinar. Hosted by FCS Compliance, the 30-minute session aims to clarify recent changes to Money Laundering Regulations and their practical impact on estate and letting agents.

The webinar, scheduled for Thursday 16 July at 1pm, comes shortly after HM Treasury announced amendments to the Money Laundering Regulations on 30 June. With further guidance expected soon, the session could help property businesses prepare for upcoming compliance obligations. Stephen Williamson, Lead AML Consultant at FCS Compliance, will guide attendees through the key changes and their implications.

Topics will include how the updated regulations affect estate agents' daily operations, along with anticipated HMRC expectations regarding risk assessments and customer due diligence. The session will particularly focus on practical adjustments that firms might need to make to their existing AML processes.

Key areas of focus

Property professionals attending the webinar can expect detailed coverage of several critical compliance areas:

  • The specific impact of regulation changes on estate and letting agents
  • How forthcoming HMRC guidance may influence AML compliance practices
  • Required updates to risk assessments, policies and procedures
  • When to review customer due diligence and enhanced due diligence measures
  • Identifying high-risk clients and transaction warning signs
  • Essential record-keeping, training and audit trail requirements

With HMRC increasing its focus on AML compliance in the property sector, the webinar may prove particularly valuable for businesses seeking to strengthen their controls. The session will offer actionable steps to help firms prepare for potential inspections whilst maintaining robust anti-money laundering processes.

Those interested in attending the free webinar can register through FCS Compliance's website. As regulatory requirements continue to evolve, staying informed about AML obligations remains crucial for all property professionals operating in the UK market.
